title: Design a Live User Count System for a Streaming Platform

transcript.text:
Design a Live User Count System for a Streaming Platform

Design a system to track and display the live user count for a video streaming platform, similar to Hotstar. The system should handle millions of concurrent users, provide near real-time updates, and be highly available and scalable. Consider: user activity tracking, real-time aggregation, scalability and performance, data consistency, suitable technologies, and fault tolerance.

blocks.md:
## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale first, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — the write firehose, the aggregation, and read fan-out — checking in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension: this is a **counting** problem at an absurd write rate. Tens of millions of people are watching at once, each device is constantly saying "I'm still here," and we have to turn that firehose of "still here" signals into one number — *per stream and globally* — that's fresh, cheap to read, and never wildly wrong. Counting is trivial; counting **distinct live humans at 5M signals/sec without melting a database** is the whole game.

**Functional (the core flows — "above the line"):**

**F1 — Track that a user is actively watching a given stream.**
Devices send a periodic "I'm alive on stream X" signal (a heartbeat) while playing.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** every phone or TV playing a match quietly pings us every few seconds to say "still watching the India game," and when those pings stop, we know they left.
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** at 50M concurrent viewers a 10-second heartbeat is ~5 million writes per second — no single database survives that, so the "write" can't be a normal DB insert.

**F2 — Compute a near-real-time live count per stream.**
For each stream, return the number of currently-active distinct users.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** under each video it says "4.2M watching now," and that number tracks reality within a few seconds.
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** "distinct users" means we can't just add 1 per heartbeat — the same person heartbeats many times, so naive addition over-counts by 10–30x. We need distinct-counting, not a running sum.

**F3 — Compute a global live count across all streams.**
A single platform-wide "X people watching right now" number.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** the homepage banner that says "58M watching on the platform" during the final.
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** it's the sum of every stream's count, recomputed continuously — a single global aggregate is a natural hot-spot and single point of serialization if done naively.

**F4 — Fan the count out to millions of viewers with low latency.**
Every watching client should see the live number update without hammering us.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** all 50M screens show a number that ticks up and down on its own, and they're not each calling our servers every second to get it.
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** if every client polls the count every 5s that's ~10M reads/sec, and pushing a 50-byte number to 50M clients once a second is ~2.5 GB/s of egress — read fan-out is as scary as the write firehose.

**Below the line (out of scope for today):** exact billing-grade view counts, per-user watch history / analytics warehouse, recommendation, DRM/auth internals, ad insertion, content delivery of the video bytes themselves (that's the CDN's job — we only count). I'll mention where they bolt on.

**Non-Functional:**

**N1 — Scale: millions of concurrent users (design target ~50M peak, headroom to 75M).**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** the system has to stand up on the single biggest day of the year — a cricket final — not just an average Tuesday.
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** the heartbeat ingest tier or the counter shard saturates at the worst possible moment and the count freezes or the app's "live" badge dies during peak — the one minute everyone's looking.

**N2 — Freshness: count is eventually consistent within ~5–10 seconds (p99).**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** the number can lag reality by a handful of seconds, but not minutes.
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** a 2-minute-stale count during a wicket makes the product feel dead and broken; the whole point is *live*.

**N3 — Accuracy: "good enough," not exact — within ~1–2% of true distinct count.**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** showing 4.21M when it's really 4.19M is totally fine; nobody can tell, and chasing exactness would cost 100x.
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** if we demanded exactness we'd need to store every live user ID in a set (~0.8 GB per global set, constant churn) and do exact-distinct math on the hot path — we'd trade a feature nobody needs for a system that can't keep up.

**N4 — Availability: 99.9% on the count read path (the displayed number).**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** about 8.7 hours of "count unavailable" per year total — and even then we degrade to a slightly stale number, we don't show an error.
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** the badge shows "—" or an error spinner on millions of screens; better to show a 30-second-old number than nothing.

**N5 — Cost: the count must be cheap per viewer.**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** we're adding a vanity number on top of a video platform — it can't cost more than the video.
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** naive designs (per-heartbeat DB write, per-client polling) cost more in infra than the streaming itself; cost is a first-class design constraint here, not an afterthought.

> 💬 "So the headline tension is: a 5-million-writes-a-second firehose on one side, a 10-million-reads-a-second fan-out on the other, and in the middle one approximate number. I'm going to spend my whole budget making that number cheap to write and cheap to read — and I'll happily trade exactness to get there."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "At its heart this is a distinct-counting problem under extreme load. Tens of millions of devices each heartbeat 'I'm watching stream X' every few seconds, and I have to turn that into one fresh number per stream and one global number. The hard parts aren't the counting — it's surviving ~5M writes a second on ingest, not over-counting the same person, and fanning the result out to 50M screens without 10M reads a second hitting my servers. The two things I'm deliberately relaxing are exactness — approximate within a percent or two is fine — and consistency — a few seconds stale is fine. Those two relaxations are what make the whole thing affordable, and I'll lean on them everywhere."

> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Before I size it — are you good with me treating this as approximate-and-eventual, Hotstar-style, or do you specifically want an exact, audit-grade count? That single answer changes the entire core, so I want to lock it."

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

**Q1 — "What's the peak concurrency I'm designing for — a few hundred thousand, or tens of millions like a cricket final?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** the entire architecture flips on this — at 100K concurrent a single Redis box counts everyone; at 50M I need a sharded, probabilistic, streaming pipeline.
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If small → a single in-memory counter / one Redis instance, done in 10 minutes. If huge → sharded ingest + stream processing + approximate counting (the real design).
- **Assumption I'll proceed with:** 50M peak concurrent, because "like Hotstar" implies the hard end.

**Q2 — "Does the count need to be exact, or is approximate within a percent or two acceptable?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** exact distinct-count means storing every live user ID and de-duping on the hot path; approximate lets me use a tiny probabilistic sketch and cut memory and CPU by ~100x.
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If exact → big in-memory sets / Redis sets, expensive, hard to shard. If approximate → HyperLogLog sketches, cheap and mergeable.
- **Assumption I'll proceed with:** approximate, ±1–2%. It's a display number.

**Q3 — "How fresh must it be — sub-second, or is 5–10 seconds of lag fine?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** sub-second forces synchronous push on every heartbeat; multi-second lag lets me batch and aggregate in windows, which is dramatically cheaper.
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If sub-second → tighter, costlier streaming + push on every change. If ~10s → micro-batch every few seconds, push deltas.
- **Assumption I'll proceed with:** 5–10s p99 freshness.

**Q4 — "Do we count distinct *users* (logged-in identity) or distinct *devices/sessions*?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** one user on phone + TV is two devices but one human; the dedup key changes the number and the privacy story.
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If users → key on user_id, dedup across devices. If devices → key on session_id, simpler, slightly higher numbers.
- **Assumption I'll proceed with:** distinct **sessions/devices** (key on session_id), which is what most "watching now" counters actually show, and note we can switch the key to user_id cheaply.

**Q5 — "Is a user 'live' purely on a heartbeat timeout, or do we get explicit leave events too?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** we can rarely trust a clean "I left" event (apps get killed, networks drop), so timeout-based liveness is the safe default — but explicit leaves make the count drop faster.
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If explicit-leave trusted → faster decrement, but must handle missed leaves. If timeout-only → simpler, but count lags downward by one window on mass exits.
- **Assumption I'll proceed with:** timeout-based liveness (a user is live if seen in the last ~30s), treat explicit leaves as an optimization hint only.

**Q6 — "Per-stream counts only, or also global, and do we need history/time-travel?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** global is just a sum of per-stream sketches if I design for mergeability; history (a graph of concurrency over time) means also writing aggregates to a time-series store.
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If live-only → keep sketches in memory/Redis, no durable history. If history too → also append per-window aggregates to a time-series DB for the "concurrency graph."
- **Assumption I'll proceed with:** both live per-stream and global, plus a lightweight per-minute history rollup (cheap, useful, and asked for implicitly by "dashboards").

**Q7 — "Single region or global audience?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** a global audience means regional ingest + a cross-region merge for the global number, which adds a replica-lag story.
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If single region → simpler. If multi-region → count locally per region, merge sketches centrally for the global figure.
- **Assumption I'll proceed with:** multi-region ingest, mergeable sketches → one global number (this is where HLL's mergeability really pays off).

> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "I'm going to assume 50M peak, approximate ±2%, ~10s fresh, dedup on session, timeout-based liveness, per-stream + global + light history, multi-region. Any of those you want to override before I size it?"

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

Gloss: **QPS** — queries per second, how many requests hit a tier each second. **Concurrent** — users connected/watching at the same instant (not per day). **Heartbeat** — a small periodic "still here" ping from a client.

| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Peak concurrent viewers | given design target | 50M | "fifty million at once" | everything is sized off this |
| Heartbeat write firehose | 50M viewers ÷ 10s heartbeat interval = 5,000,000/s | ~5M/s | "five million writes a second" | **THE number** — kills any per-write DB; forces ingest buffer + aggregation |
| Heartbeat firehose (30s interval) | 50M ÷ 30s = 1,666,667/s | ~1.7M/s | "even at a 30-second beat, ~1.7M/s" | heartbeat interval is a cost dial we tune |
| Connect surge at event start | 50M connecting over ~120s = 416,667/s | ~400K/s | "four hundred thousand new connections a second" | gateway must absorb a thundering-herd connect storm |
| Naive read fan-out (polling) | 50M clients ÷ 5s poll = 10,000,000/s | ~10M/s | "ten million reads a second if they poll" | kills polling → forces push / CDN-cached count |
| Push egress (broadcast count) | 50 bytes × 50M clients × 1/s = 2.5 GB/s | ~2.5 GB/s | "a couple gigs a second just to broadcast a number" | push deltas, coalesce, cache at edge — not per-client compute |
| Redis-shard count for firehose | 5M ops/s ÷ ~150K ops/s per node = 33 | ~30–40 nodes | "a couple dozen shards if I went pure Redis" | favors pre-aggregation in stream layer over Redis-per-heartbeat |
| Exact-set memory (rejected path) | 50M ids × 16 bytes = 0.8 GB per global set, churning | ~0.8 GB | "most of a gig of IDs, constantly rewritten" | justifies HLL instead of exact sets |
| HLL sketch memory | ~12 KB per counter × 10K streams = 120 MB | ~120 MB | "the entire counting state fits in a fraction of a gig" | approximate counting is basically free on memory |

The **one number that forces the design** is **~5M heartbeat writes/sec**. Flip threshold: a single well-tuned aggregation node handles ~100–200K events/sec, and one Redis node ~150K ops/s — so anywhere north of a few hundred thousand writes/sec, per-heartbeat persistence is dead and you *must* pre-aggregate in a streaming layer before anything durable. We're 25x past that line, so the architecture is "buffer the firehose → aggregate in memory → emit small deltas," not "write each heartbeat somewhere."

Storage is a non-issue: the live counting state is ~120 MB of sketches; even a year of per-minute history rollups is a few tens of GB. The cost and complexity are entirely in **throughput**, not bytes.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The number that drives everything is about five million heartbeats a second at peak. That's twenty-five times what a single aggregation node or Redis box can take, so per-heartbeat writes are off the table — I have to buffer the firehose and aggregate it in memory before it ever touches durable storage. On the read side, if clients polled I'd be looking at ten million reads a second and a couple gigs a second of egress, so I push instead of letting them pull. Storage itself is a rounding error — the whole counting state is around a hundred megs of sketches. So this is a throughput problem on both ends, and I'll spend the entire budget there."

## 4. Core Entities

This is a first draft of the nouns; I'll add full field definitions in §7.

**Heartbeat (event)** — the atomic input: "session S is alive on stream X at time T." It owns *liveness evidence*. It's high-volume and disposable — we aggregate it and throw the raw event away.
- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** like a turnstile click at a stadium gate — one click means "a body just passed," we care about the running count, not about keeping every individual click forever.
- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "Do you store every heartbeat?" → No — that's 5M rows/sec. We fold each heartbeat into an in-memory sketch within seconds and drop the raw event; only rolled-up aggregates are (optionally) persisted.

**Session** — one watching device/connection, identified by session_id, the **dedup key** for "distinct." It owns *who counts as one viewer*.
- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** like a wristband at a festival — one wristband = one attendee, and even if you walk past the counter ten times you're still one wristband.
- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "Same person on phone and TV — one or two?" → With session as the key, two. If they want human-distinct, we re-key on user_id; the machinery is identical, only the key changes.

**Stream** — a single live video (a match, a show). It owns *the scope of a count* and carries the per-stream sketch + current count.
- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** think of each stream like a separate room with a people-counter on its door; the global count is just the sum of all the door counters.
- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "One stream has 90% of all viewers (the final) — hot-spot?" → Yes, the marquee stream is a single hot key; we shard *its* sketch across partitions and merge, so no one node owns the whole final.

**CountSketch (per stream + global)** — the **HyperLogLog** sketch: a tiny probabilistic structure that estimates distinct count. It owns *the answer*.
- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** like estimating crowd size from how packed the densest patch is, instead of counting every head — close enough, almost free.
- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "Why not an exact set?" → A set of 50M IDs is ~0.8 GB and churns constantly; an HLL is ~12 KB for the same job at ±2%, and crucially HLLs *merge* — so global = merge of all per-stream sketches.

**CountSnapshot** — the published, read-optimized value: `{stream_id, count, as_of_ts}`, what clients actually read. It owns *the cheap read*.
- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** like a scoreboard that's updated every few seconds — everyone reads the board, nobody bothers the scorekeeper.
- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "What do 50M clients actually hit?" → This snapshot, served from cache/edge and pushed over a fan-out channel — never the sketches or the firehose.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The nouns are simple: a Heartbeat is one 'I'm alive' event, a Session is one device and it's my dedup key, a Stream is one video and the scope of a count, the CountSketch is a HyperLogLog that actually holds the estimate, and the CountSnapshot is the cheap published number clients read. The one to flag is the CountSketch — choosing a probabilistic, *mergeable* sketch instead of an exact set is the decision the whole design hangs on, because it makes global-count just a merge and makes the memory basically free. I'll put real fields on each in the data-model section."

## 5. API / Interface

Default is a lightweight ingest endpoint for writes and a push channel for reads — REST for control, a persistent connection for the live number.

**1) Heartbeat ingest — `POST /v1/heartbeat`**
- **Why POST:** it records new liveness evidence (creates/refreshes state); it's not cacheable and not idempotent-by-URL, so GET is wrong.
- **Key request fields:**
  - `streamId: string` — which stream this session is watching; the scope we count under.
  - `sessionId: string` — the dedup identity; server *derives* this from the connection/JWT where possible so a client can't spoof millions of fake sessions to inflate the count.
  - `clientTs: int` — client timestamp (advisory only; server stamps authoritative receive time).
  - **Absent on purpose:** no `count`, no `userId` trusted from body — the client never sends the count (obviously), and identity comes from the auth token, not a body field, so it can't be forged.
- **Concrete example:**
```
// Request (sent every ~10s while playing)
POST /v1/heartbeat
{ "streamId": "stream_ipl_final", "sessionId": "sess_abc123", "clientTs": 1718800000 }

// Response 202 Accepted
{ "ack": true, "nextHeartbeatSec": 10 }
```
- **Key design decision:** it returns **202 Accepted**, not 200 with data — ingest is fire-and-forget into a buffer; we *never* do a DB write or a count read on this path. `nextHeartbeatSec` lets the server **back-pressure** by telling clients to slow down during overload (a cheap, powerful load-shedding lever).

**2) Get current count (cold/fallback) — `GET /v1/streams/{streamId}/count`**
- **Why GET:** pure read, no state change, and **cacheable** — that's the whole point, we want a CDN/edge to answer it.
- **Response fields:** `count: int` (the displayed number), `asOfTs: int` (so clients know freshness), `confidence` (optional ± band).
- **Concrete example:**
```
GET /v1/streams/stream_ipl_final/count

// Response 200  (Cache-Control: max-age=3, stale-while-revalidate=10)
{ "streamId": "stream_ipl_final", "count": 4210000, "asOfTs": 1718800003 }
```
- **Key design decision:** short-TTL edge caching (`max-age` ~3s). 50M clients hitting this collapse to a handful of origin reads per stream per few seconds — this is the polling fallback for clients that can't hold a push connection.

**3) Live count subscription (the main read path) — `WS /v1/streams/{streamId}/live` (WebSocket / SSE)**
- **Why a persistent connection:** push beats poll at this scale — one open channel, server sends a new number only when it changes, instead of 50M clients asking every few seconds.
- **Message example:**
```
// Server → client, only on change
{ "type": "count", "streamId": "stream_ipl_final", "count": 4212500, "asOfTs": 1718800006 }
```
- **Key design decision:** server pushes **coalesced deltas** (at most ~1 update/sec/stream, batched), so a fast-moving number doesn't mean fast-moving traffic. The same connection that carries the count can carry the heartbeat, killing two birds.

> 💬 "Notice the write path returns 202 and the read path is a push channel — I never let a client's heartbeat trigger a count read, and I never let a client poll for the count. Both would multiply 50M by something terrifying."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Three surfaces: clients POST a heartbeat every ten seconds and get an instant 202 — that's fire-and-forget into a buffer, no DB, no read. To see the number, they hold a WebSocket and I push coalesced updates about once a second only when it changes. And there's a cacheable GET as a fallback for clients that can't hold a socket, sitting behind a 3-second edge cache so 50M of them collapse to a few origin hits. Identity comes from the token, never the body, so nobody can stuff the ballot box."

## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time)

Color palette used in every diagram: blue = clients, green = our services, amber = queues/streams, grey = datastores, purple = external/edge.

### 6.1 "Track that a user is actively watching a stream" (F1)

The user is just watching video; in the background their client emits a heartbeat every ~10s. The interesting part is absorbing 5M of these per second **without** touching a database.

**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Edge / API Gateway | The front door that terminates connections and load-balances heartbeats | Must absorb ~400K new conns/s and 5M req/s, do TLS, shed load | Not a single LB box (one box can't take 5M/s); not direct-to-service (no back-pressure point) | One more hop of latency (fine, ~ms) |
| Ingest service (stateless) | A thin tier that validates a heartbeat and drops it on a queue | Needs to be horizontally trivial — no state, just enqueue | Not write-to-DB here (5M writes/s impossible); not aggregate-here (would make ingest stateful/hot) | Adds a queue hop before counting |
| Kafka (event log) | A durable append-only log that buffers the firehose so nothing is lost if consumers lag | Decouples 5M/s spiky ingest from steady aggregation; replayable | Not Redis list (not durable, no replay); not direct RPC to aggregators (couples ingest to consumer speed) | ~hundreds of ms of buffer latency |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "The gateway is just the front door — it terminates the connection and spreads load, and critically it's where I can shed load by telling clients to heartbeat less often. The ingest service is deliberately brain-dead: validate, stamp a server time, and drop the event onto Kafka. Kafka here is a durable log — think of it as a conveyor belt that holds the firehose so that if my counters fall behind, nothing's lost and they can catch up. I rejected writing heartbeats to a database because five million writes a second is a non-starter, and I rejected aggregating inside ingest because that would make the front door stateful and hot."

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **API Gateway** — the entry layer that terminates client connections, does TLS and routing, and load-balances. Here it absorbs the connect surge and is our load-shedding lever.
> **Kafka** — a durable, replayable append-only log that buffers a stream of events between fast producers and slower consumers. Here it holds the heartbeat firehose so aggregation can run at its own pace without losing data.
> **Heartbeat** — a small periodic "still watching" ping from each client. Here it's the only signal of liveness; absence of it for ~30s means the viewer left.

**DIAGRAM — Heartbeat ingest (write path):**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Client[Client app]
    Client -->|"1: POST heartbeat 10s"| GW
    subgraph Ingest["Write / Ingest path"]
        GW[API Gateway]
        GW -->|"2: validate + stamp"| ING[Ingest service]
        ING -->|"3: enqueue by streamId"| K[[Kafka heartbeat log]]
    end
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class Client client
    class GW,ING svc
    class K queue
```

**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
1. **Client POSTs a heartbeat** every ~10s. It's fire-and-forget; the client doesn't wait on a count. *Why here:* the gateway is the only thing built to take 5M/s. *State change:* none yet — just a request in flight. *What goes wrong:* gateway overload → it returns a higher `nextHeartbeatSec`, clients slow down, load sheds gracefully.
2. **Gateway validates and stamps** server receive-time and derives sessionId from the token. *Why here:* authoritative timestamp + anti-spoof must be server-side. *State change:* request is now trusted and enriched. *What goes wrong:* bad/forged token → reject before it pollutes the count.
3. **Ingest enqueues to Kafka, partitioned by streamId.** *Why here:* partitioning by stream means all of one stream's heartbeats land on the same partitions, so a consumer can aggregate that stream locally. *State change:* event durably buffered, ingest is done. *What goes wrong:* Kafka partition slow → events buffer (that's its job), counters lag by seconds, nothing is lost.

> 💬 "The key insight: the write path never reads or computes the count — it just durably parks the heartbeat on a log partitioned by stream, so counting can happen downstream at its own pace."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "On the write path, every client heartbeats every ten seconds into the gateway, which validates and stamps it, and ingest drops it onto Kafka partitioned by stream. Kafka's a durable log — a conveyor belt that decouples the spiky five-million-a-second firehose from the steady work of counting. Nothing is counted yet; I've just made sure no heartbeat is lost and that each stream's events are grouped together for the next stage. The trade-off is a few hundred milliseconds of buffering latency, which is nothing against my ten-second freshness budget."

### 6.2 "Compute a near-real-time live count per stream" (F2)

Now the interesting part: turn a partition of heartbeats into a distinct count per stream, cheaply, and de-dupe the same session heartbeating repeatedly.

**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stream processor (Flink) | A framework that reads the Kafka log and maintains running, windowed aggregates in memory | Need stateful, windowed distinct-counting at firehose rate with fault-tolerant checkpoints | Not batch jobs (too slow for 10s freshness); not hand-rolled consumers (we'd reinvent windowing + checkpointing) | Operational complexity of a Flink cluster |
| HyperLogLog sketch | A tiny structure that *estimates* distinct count and can be merged | Distinct-count at ±2% in ~12 KB instead of a 0.8 GB set; **mergeable** → enables global | Not exact Redis SET (0.8 GB, churns, doesn't merge cheaply); not a running sum (over-counts 10–30x) | ±1–2% error (acceptable per N3) |
| Redis (published counts) | An in-memory store holding the current count + sketch per stream | Sub-ms reads/writes for the published snapshot and the fan-out layer to read | Not Postgres (can't take the snapshot update rate per stream); not in-Flink-only (need an external read surface) | Another store to operate |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "Flink is a stream processor — it reads the Kafka log and keeps running totals in memory with built-in windowing and fault-tolerant checkpoints, so if a node dies it restores its counters and keeps going. Inside Flink, for each stream I keep a HyperLogLog — a probabilistic sketch that estimates how many *distinct* sessions I've seen using a sliding window, in about twelve kilobytes, at one-to-two-percent error. I rejected an exact set because storing every live ID is most of a gigabyte that churns every window, and a plain running sum is useless because the same session heartbeats over and over. Every few seconds Flink writes the current estimate to Redis, which is the cheap surface the fan-out layer reads."

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Flink (stream processor)** — a system that continuously reads an event stream and maintains windowed, stateful aggregates with fault-tolerant checkpoints. Here it does the actual distinct-counting per stream in sliding time windows.
> **HyperLogLog (HLL)** — a probabilistic sketch that estimates the number of distinct items in tiny fixed memory and can be *merged* with other sketches. Here it gives a ±2% live count per stream in ~12 KB and lets us sum streams into a global count by merging.
> **Sliding window** — counting only events seen in the last N seconds (e.g. 30s), so the count reflects *currently* live sessions and naturally drops when heartbeats stop. Here it's how "live" decays without explicit leave events.
> **Redis** — an in-memory key-value store with sub-millisecond reads/writes. Here it holds the published per-stream count and global count for the fan-out layer to read.

    K -->|"1: consume by partition"| F[Flink stream processor]
    subgraph Agg["Aggregation"]
        F -->|"2: update HLL sliding window"| HLL[Per-stream HLL state]
        HLL -->|"3: estimate every ~2s"| F
    end
    F -->|"4: write count + sketch"| R[(Redis published counts)]

**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
1. **Flink consumes its assigned partitions.** *Why here:* partition-by-stream means each Flink task owns whole streams and can count them without cross-talk. *State change:* events flow into operator state. *What goes wrong:* a task dies → Flink restores from its last checkpoint and replays from Kafka offset, no double-count beyond the window.
2. **Update the per-stream HLL over a sliding window** (insert hash of sessionId). *Why here:* HLL de-dupes repeated heartbeats by construction and the window decays stale sessions. *State change:* the sketch now reflects distinct live sessions in the last ~30s. *What goes wrong:* a hot stream (the final) overloads one task → we sub-shard that stream's key across tasks and merge sketches.
3. **Estimate the count every ~2s** from the sketch. *Why here:* we read the cheap estimate on a timer, not per-event. *State change:* a fresh integer count produced. *What goes wrong:* estimate error spikes at tiny counts → we fall back to exact small-set counting under a few thousand.
4. **Write count (+ sketch for merging) to Redis.** *Why here:* Redis is the sub-ms read surface for fan-out. *State change:* published snapshot updated. *What goes wrong:* Redis node down → fail over to replica; fan-out serves last-known value (stale, not broken).

> 💬 "The whole trick is the HyperLogLog: it de-dupes the same person heartbeating ten times *and* shrinks 50M IDs into 12 KB *and* lets me merge streams into a global number — three problems, one structure."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Flink reads each stream's heartbeats off Kafka and keeps a HyperLogLog per stream over a thirty-second sliding window. The sketch does three jobs at once: it ignores the same session pinging repeatedly, it forgets sessions whose heartbeats stopped, and it estimates the distinct count in about twelve kilobytes at a couple percent error. Every two seconds I read the estimate and write it to Redis, which is the cheap surface everything reads from. If the final is so hot it overloads one Flink task, I split that one stream's key across several tasks and merge their sketches — that's the move that keeps the marquee stream from being a single melting point."

### 6.3 "Compute a global live count across all streams" (F3)

The global number is the platform-wide banner. The interesting part: it must NOT be a single serialized counter everyone increments — that's a classic hot-spot.

**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Global merge job (Flink) | A small job that periodically merges all per-stream HLL sketches into one | HLLs merge losslessly → global = merge of parts, no central counter | Not a single atomic global counter (single point of serialization + hot-spot at 5M/s); not summing integer counts (double-counts users on multiple streams) | Global lags per-stream by one merge tick (~2–5s) |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "For the global number I deliberately avoid a single counter that every event increments — that's a hot-spot and a single point of serialization that would melt at this rate. Because HyperLogLogs merge, the global count is just the union of all the per-stream sketches, computed every few seconds by a small merge job. And merging sketches, not summing integers, correctly handles someone watching two streams — the union counts them once. The cost is the global number trails the per-stream numbers by one merge tick, which is well inside my freshness budget."

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **HLL merge** — combining two HyperLogLog sketches into one that estimates the distinct union, with no loss. Here it turns "global count" from a hot single counter into a cheap periodic union of per-stream sketches.
> **Hot-spot / single point of serialization** — one key or counter that every request must touch, which caps throughput and becomes a failure point. Here we avoid it entirely by merging sketches instead of incrementing one number.

**DIAGRAM:**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[(Redis per-stream sketches)]
    R -->|"1: read all sketches"| M[Global merge job]
    M -->|"2: HLL union every ~3s"| M
    M -->|"3: write global count"| RG[(Redis global count)]
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class M svc
    class R,RG db
```
↳ reuses existing: Redis (from §6.2), Flink runtime (from §6.2)

**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
1. **Read all per-stream sketches** from Redis (a few thousand small blobs). *Why here:* sketches are tiny (12 KB), so reading them all is cheap. *State change:* sketches loaded into the merge job. *What goes wrong:* a stream's sketch missing → skip it; global is approximate anyway, one stream's omission is within tolerance.
2. **Merge (union) all sketches** into one global HLL and estimate. *Why here:* union de-dupes cross-stream viewers correctly. *State change:* one global estimate produced. *What goes wrong:* merge job dies → last global value served from Redis until it restarts (seconds).
3. **Write the global count** to a dedicated Redis key. *State change:* global banner value refreshed. *What goes wrong:* nothing new — same fan-out/fallback as per-stream.

> 💬 "Global isn't a counter — it's a union of sketches. That one design choice turns the scariest hot-spot in the system into a cheap periodic job."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The global number is the trap question — the naive answer is one counter every heartbeat increments, which is a single melting point at five million a second. Instead, because HyperLogLogs merge, I just union all the per-stream sketches every few seconds into one global sketch and read its estimate. Merging also correctly counts a person watching two streams only once. It trails the per-stream counts by a few seconds, which is fine."

### 6.4 "Fan the count out to millions of viewers" (F4)

Getting one number to 50M screens without 10M reads/sec or 2.5 GB/s of waste. The interesting part is the read amplification.

**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fan-out / push layer | A tier of servers holding millions of WebSocket/SSE connections, pushing count deltas | Push beats poll: send only on change, coalesced ~1/s, instead of 10M polls/s | Not client polling (10M reads/s, 2.5 GB/s); not per-client compute (each conn just relays a shared value) | Must manage millions of long-lived connections |
| Edge CDN cache | Caches the GET count response near users for a few seconds | Collapses millions of fallback reads into a few origin hits per stream | Not origin-serves-all (would re-create the read firehose) | Up to ~3s extra staleness on the cached path |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "The fan-out layer holds the long-lived connections and pushes a new number only when it changes, coalesced to about once a second — so a number that ticks rapidly doesn't mean rapid traffic; every connection just relays the same shared value. For clients that can't hold a socket, there's a cacheable GET behind a CDN with a three-second TTL, which collapses millions of polls into a handful of origin reads per stream. I rejected plain polling outright — it's ten million reads a second and gigabytes a second of egress for a single number."

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **WebSocket / SSE** — a persistent connection that lets the server push messages to the client without the client asking. Here it carries live count updates so clients never poll.
> **Fan-out (push) layer** — servers that hold many client connections and broadcast a shared value to all of them. Here it turns one updated count into millions of pushes cheaply by relaying the same value.
> **CDN edge cache** — caches a response geographically close to users for a short time. Here it absorbs the polling-fallback reads so origin sees almost none.

**DIAGRAM — Read / fan-out path:**

    Client -.->|"3: fallback GET"| CDN[CDN edge cache]
    CDN -.->|"4: miss -> origin read 3s TTL"| RG

**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
1. **Fan-out servers subscribe to count changes** in Redis (pub/sub or short poll of Redis, which is cheap and internal). *Why here:* one internal subscriber per stream per fan-out node, not per client. *State change:* fan-out holds the latest value. *What goes wrong:* Redis blip → push last-known value, mark slightly stale.
2. **Push a coalesced delta to all subscribed clients** (~1/s max). *Why here:* clients share one value; we broadcast once. *State change:* 50M screens update. *What goes wrong:* a fan-out node dies → its clients reconnect to another node (the count is stateless/shared, so reconnection is cheap).
3. **Fallback clients GET the count**, served by CDN. *Why here:* not every client can hold a socket. *State change:* none. *What goes wrong:* CDN cold → one origin read per stream per 3s.
4. **CDN miss reads origin** (Redis) with a 3s TTL. *State change:* edge cache filled. *What goes wrong:* origin down → CDN serves stale-while-revalidate, users still see a number.

> 💬 "Push, don't poll, and let everyone share one value — that's how one number reaches fifty million screens for almost nothing."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "On the read side I refuse to let clients poll — that's ten million reads a second for one number. Instead a fan-out tier holds the WebSocket connections and pushes a coalesced update about once a second only when the number moves, and every connection just relays the same shared value, so it's cheap. Clients that can't hold a socket fall back to a cacheable GET behind a CDN with a three-second TTL, which collapses millions of those into a few origin hits. If a fan-out node dies, clients just reconnect elsewhere because there's no per-client state to lose."

## 7. Data Model & Storage

**PART A — ENTITY TABLE**

| Entity | Key fields (PK / shard key annotated) | Chosen store | Shard / partition key | Consistency guarantee |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Heartbeat (event) | `streamId` *(partition key)*, `sessionId`, `serverTs`, (ttl-ish, never stored long) | Kafka (log) | `streamId` | Durable, at-least-once; dropped after aggregation |
| Per-stream sketch | `streamId` *(PK)*, `hll_blob`, `windowEndTs` | Redis (in-mem) | `streamId` | Eventual, ~2s freshness |
| Per-stream count | `streamId` *(PK)*, `count`, `asOfTs` | Redis | `streamId` | Eventual, read-mostly |
| Global count | `global` *(PK)*, `count`, `asOfTs` | Redis | single key (small) | Eventual, ~3–5s |
| History rollup | `(streamId, minuteTs)` *(PK / shard key)* | Time-series DB (e.g. ClickHouse/Cassandra) | `streamId` + time | Eventual, append-only, durable |

**PART B — STORAGE DECISION CARDS**

**🗄️ Kafka — used for: the heartbeat firehose (raw events in flight)**
1. **What it is (plain words):** a durable, append-only log — a conveyor belt that stores a stream of events in order and lets multiple consumers read at their own pace, replaying if needed.
2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces it:** we have a spiky 5M-events/sec firehose that must be decoupled from steady aggregation; we need durability (don't lose heartbeats if a counter lags) and replay (a crashed Flink task re-reads from its offset). Partitioning by streamId co-locates a stream's events.
3. **What we considered instead and why we rejected each:**
   - **Redis list / Pub-Sub** — fast but not durable and no replay; a consumer hiccup loses heartbeats and corrupts the count.
   - **Direct RPC ingest → aggregator** — couples ingest throughput to consumer speed; a slow aggregator back-pressures the front door at 5M/s.
   - **Kinesis/PubSub (managed)** — totally fine; same model. I'd pick the managed one in a cloud shop to cut ops.
4. **Trade-off we accept:** a few hundred ms of buffering latency and a Kafka cluster to operate — cheap against the 10s freshness budget.
5. **🗣️ How to say it out loud:** "I put a durable log like Kafka between ingest and counting so the five-million-a-second firehose is decoupled and replayable. If my counters fall behind, nothing's lost — they just catch up from where they left off. I partition by stream so each stream's events stay together for counting."

**🗄️ Redis — used for: published counts + sketches + fan-out read surface**
1. **What it is (plain words):** an in-memory key-value store with sub-millisecond reads and writes, and built-in HyperLogLog support.
2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces it:** the fan-out tier and the cacheable GET need a sub-ms read of "current count," updated every couple seconds per stream; Redis also natively does HLL add/merge, so the sketches live where they're used.
3. **What we considered instead and why we rejected each:**
   - **Postgres** — can't take per-stream snapshot updates every 2s across thousands of streams plus the read load; wrong tool for hot, ephemeral counters.
   - **In-Flink state only** — Flink holds the authoritative sketch, but we still need an external, cheap read surface for fan-out/CDN; Redis is that surface.
   - **Memcached** — no native HLL, no persistence/replication story as clean; Redis gives us sketches + replicas.
4. **Trade-off we accept:** it's another store to run and it's memory-bound — but the whole counting state is ~120 MB, so that's trivial.
5. **🗣️ How to say it out loud:** "Redis is my hot read surface — sub-millisecond, and it speaks HyperLogLog natively, so the sketch lives exactly where fan-out reads it. Flink is the source of truth; Redis is the published, cheap-to-read copy. I didn't use Postgres because counters that update every two seconds across thousands of streams aren't a relational-DB job."

**🗄️ Time-series DB (ClickHouse / Cassandra) — used for: per-minute history rollups**
1. **What it is (plain words):** a store optimized for append-only, timestamped rows and fast range queries over time.
2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces it:** the "concurrency over time" graph is a time-range scan per stream; columnar/TS stores answer that cheaply, and the write rate (per-minute per-stream) is tiny.
3. **What we considered instead and why we rejected each:**
   - **Keep history in Redis** — memory is precious and history is cold; wrong tier.
   - **Postgres** — works at this small write rate but range-scans over time are less efficient than a columnar/TS store at scale.
4. **Trade-off we accept:** it's eventually consistent and a minute granular — fine, history doesn't need to be live or exact.
5. **🗣️ How to say it out loud:** "History is cheap and cold — per-minute rollups into a time-series store give me the concurrency graph without bloating Redis. It's append-only and a minute granular, which is all a trend chart needs."

**PART C — Per-operation consistency summary**

| Operation | Store | Consistency level | Why that level is right here |
|---|---|---|---|
| Record heartbeat | Kafka | Durable, at-least-once | Can't lose liveness evidence; dup heartbeats are harmless (HLL de-dupes) |
| Update per-stream sketch/count | Flink → Redis | Eventual (~2s) | Display number; a couple seconds stale is invisible to users |
| Read live count (push) | Redis → fan-out | Eventual (~1–2s) | Same — fresh enough, cheap |
| Read live count (fallback GET) | Redis → CDN | Eventual (~3s) | Edge cache trades 3s staleness for collapsing the read firehose |
| Global count | Redis (merge) | Eventual (~3–5s) | Union of sketches; trailing a few seconds is fine |
| Read history graph | Time-series DB | Eventual, durable | Trend data, not live |

There's no read-your-own-writes requirement here — a viewer doesn't need to see *their own* heartbeat reflected instantly; they need the aggregate to be roughly right. That's a deliberate relaxation that buys us the whole cheap design.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Storage splits three ways. Kafka holds the raw firehose durably and replayably — that's the only place I keep heartbeats, and only briefly. Redis holds the live answer: per-stream sketches and counts plus the global number, sub-millisecond to read, with native HyperLogLog. And a time-series store holds per-minute history for the trend graph. Everything is eventually consistent within a few seconds, and I lean on that hard — I considered making the count strongly consistent and exact, but storing every live ID in a set is most of a gigabyte that churns constantly for a number nobody needs to the unit, so I rejected it."

## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great

🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to the two firehoses — 5M writes in, 10M reads out — and ask "what makes this number cheap to write and cheap to read?" Every answer here is a variation on *buffer, aggregate, approximate, push*.

### How do we count distinct live users at 5M heartbeats/sec without over-counting or melting the DB?

**#### Bad: Per-heartbeat DB increment**
**🗣️ What this is in plain words:** every time a phone says "I'm here," we write a row or bump a counter in a database.
**Approach:** on each heartbeat, `INSERT` a (session, stream, ts) row or `INCR` a per-stream counter; compute count by querying.
**Why people try this:** it's the obvious first thought — a heartbeat is an event, events go in the DB.
**⚠️ What breaks:** 5M writes/sec obliterates any OLTP database (a tuned Postgres does low tens of thousands of writes/sec — we're 100–200x over). And `INCR` over-counts wildly: the same session heartbeats every 10s, so a counter counts one viewer 6x/minute. A `SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT session)` over a firehose table is a full scan that never finishes.
**🔁 What forces the upgrade:** the write rate alone is fatal; we must stop touching durable storage per heartbeat.

**#### Good: Buffer + windowed exact-distinct in memory**
**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** Bad wrote per heartbeat to a DB and used a sum that double-counts. Good moves counting off the DB entirely and de-dupes properly with a windowed *set*.
**🗣️ What this is in plain words:** park heartbeats on a log, and in memory keep a set of "session IDs seen in the last 30 seconds" per stream — the set's size is the live count.
**Approach:** ingest → Kafka (a durable log that buffers the firehose). Flink (a stream processor that maintains windowed in-memory state) keeps a **set** of sessionIds per stream over a 30s sliding window; count = set size. Repeated heartbeats hit the same set member (de-dup by construction); sessions whose heartbeats stop fall out of the window.
**Why this seemed reasonable:** sets give *exact* distinct counts and naturally de-dupe.
**⚠️ What breaks:** memory. A set of 50M session IDs is ~0.8 GB *for the global set alone*, churning every window; across a hot stream it concentrates on one task and the GC/heap pressure is brutal. Exact sets also don't *merge* cheaply, so the global count becomes a giant union of huge sets every few seconds.
**🔁 What forces the upgrade:** memory and merge cost — we want the de-dup and windowing of a set without paying 0.8 GB and without expensive global unions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[[Kafka log]] -->|"consume"| F[Flink]
    F -->|"add sessionId to 30s set"| S[Per-stream Set]
    S -->|"count = set size"| F
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class K queue
    class F svc
    class S db
```

**#### Great: HyperLogLog sketches, sub-sharded for hot streams**
**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** Good's exact sets cost ~0.8 GB and don't merge, making the global count expensive and the hot stream a heap bomb. Great swaps the exact set for a probabilistic sketch that's ~12 KB and merges losslessly.
**🗣️ What this is in plain words:** instead of remembering every viewer's ID, keep a tiny "fingerprint" that *estimates* how many distinct viewers there are — close enough, and you can add fingerprints together to get the total.
**Approach:** per stream, maintain a **HyperLogLog** (a probabilistic distinct-count sketch: ~12 KB, ±~2%, mergeable) over the sliding window — add `hash(sessionId)` per heartbeat. For a **hot stream** (the cricket final at 90% of traffic), sub-shard its key across N Flink tasks (`streamId#0..N`), each with its own HLL, and **merge** them for the stream's count — so no single task owns the whole final. Global count = merge of all per-stream sketches. Flink **checkpoints** sketches (periodic fault-tolerant snapshots) so a crash restores state and replays from the Kafka offset.
**Why this is great:** de-dup + windowing + tiny memory + mergeability, all at once. The hot-key relief and the global count fall out of the *same* mergeable property.

**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** exact set = 50M × 16 B ≈ **0.8 GB** and grows with viewers; HLL = **12 KB** flat, independent of viewer count, at ±2%. That's ~**65,000x** less memory for an error nobody can see. One Flink task does ~100–200K events/s; the final alone is millions/s → sub-shard across ~20–40 tasks and merge.

**✅ Failure matrix:**

| Scenario | What happens | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Flink task crashes | In-flight window state lost | Restore from last checkpoint, replay from Kafka offset; at-most one window of skew |
| Duplicate heartbeats (retries) | Same session added twice | HLL is idempotent on identical input — no effect |
| Hot stream overloads one task | Counting falls behind for the final | Sub-shard the stream key across tasks, merge sketches |
| Kafka lag spikes | Counts lag reality | Counts trail by lag seconds (still within budget); Kafka durably buffers, no loss |
| Tiny count (<~1000) | HLL relative error is large at small N | Fall back to exact small set below a threshold |
| Whole region down | That region's heartbeats stop | Global = merge of surviving regions' sketches; degrade, don't fail |

**🎙️ Script:** "The heart of it is HyperLogLog. Instead of remembering fifty million viewer IDs in a set — that's most of a gigabyte that churns every window — I keep a twelve-kilobyte sketch per stream that estimates the distinct count within a couple percent. It de-dupes the same session pinging repeatedly, it forgets sessions when their heartbeats stop because it's windowed, and best of all sketches *merge*, so the global count is just the union of all the per-stream sketches and a hot stream can be split across tasks and merged back. Flink checkpoints the sketches so a crash just restores and replays from Kafka. I'm trading exactness for a sixty-five-thousand-fold memory win — and the error is invisible on a number we show as '4.2 million.'"

**🧠 If they ask "isn't HLL inaccurate?":** "It's ±2% at standard precision, which on a 4.2M count is noise no human perceives. And below about a thousand viewers, where the relative error would matter, I switch to an exact set — so small streams are precise and big streams are cheap."

### How do we serve one number to 50M screens without a 10M-reads/sec firehose?

**#### Bad: Clients poll GET every few seconds**
**🗣️ Plain words:** every screen asks our server "what's the count now?" every few seconds.
**Approach:** client calls `GET /count` on a timer.
**Why people try this:** simplest possible — it's just a request.
**⚠️ What breaks:** 50M ÷ 5s = **10M reads/sec** for one tiny number, plus ~2.5 GB/s of egress. Origin and bandwidth costs explode for a vanity figure.
**🔁 What forces the upgrade:** read amplification — we must decouple "number changes" from "clients ask."

**#### Good: CDN-cached GET with short TTL**
**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** Bad sent every poll to origin. Good puts a 3-second edge cache in front so millions of polls collapse to a few origin reads.
**🗣️ Plain words:** keep the answer on caches near users for 3 seconds, so most polls never reach us.
**Approach:** `GET /count` with `Cache-Control: max-age=3, stale-while-revalidate=10`; CDN serves the cached value.
**⚠️ What breaks:** still wasteful — clients keep polling even when nothing changed, and 3s TTL on a fast-ticking number feels laggy; mobile radios burn battery polling.
**🔁 What forces the upgrade:** we still pay for polls that mostly return the same value; push only on change is strictly better for a live number.

**#### Great: Push fan-out over persistent connections + CDN fallback**
**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** Good still polls and caps freshness at the TTL. Great holds open connections and pushes a new number only when it actually changes.
**🗣️ Plain words:** every screen keeps a thin open line to us; when the number moves, we shout the new value down all the lines at once.
**Approach:** a **fan-out tier** holds millions of **WebSocket/SSE** connections; it subscribes once per stream to Redis and **pushes coalesced deltas** (≤1/s) to all subscribers — every connection relays the same shared value, so cost scales with *streams*, not naively with clients. The cacheable GET remains as a fallback for clients that can't hold a socket. Connections are stateless (the value is shared), so a dead fan-out node just means clients reconnect elsewhere.
**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** poll = 10M reads/s. Push = the number changes ~1/s/stream; per fan-out node holding ~100K conns, broadcasting one value is one read + a memory fan-out, not 100K origin reads. Origin load drops from 10M/s to ~(streams ÷ TTL), i.e. thousands/s.

**✅ Failure matrix:**

| Scenario | What happens | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Fan-out node dies | ~100K clients drop | They reconnect to another node; shared value, no state lost |
| Redis blip | No fresh value to push | Push last-known value flagged slightly stale; resume on recovery |
| Client can't hold socket | No push | Falls back to CDN-cached GET |
| Reconnect storm (node death) | Surge of reconnects | Gateway jittered backoff + spread across nodes |
| CDN cold for a stream | Origin read | One read per stream per 3s — trivial |

**🎙️ Script:** "Polling is the trap — fifty million screens asking every few seconds is ten million reads a second for one number. So I push instead: a fan-out tier holds the WebSocket connections, subscribes once per stream to Redis, and broadcasts a coalesced update about once a second only when the number moves. Every connection relays the same shared value, so my cost scales with the number of streams, not naively with viewers. Clients that can't hold a socket fall back to a three-second CDN-cached GET. And because there's no per-client state, a fan-out node dying just means its clients reconnect somewhere else."

**🧠 If they ask "how do you hold 50M WebSockets?":** "Horizontally — each node holds ~100–200K connections, so 50M is a few hundred nodes behind a connection-aware load balancer. The connections are cheap because they're idle except for ~1 push/sec; the hard part is the connect *surge* at event start, which I smooth with jittered reconnect backoff and the gateway's back-pressure signal."

### How do we keep the count correct when components fail or duplicate?

**#### Great: At-least-once + idempotent sketch + checkpointing**
**🗣️ Plain words:** assume everything is delivered at least once (maybe twice) and design counting so duplicates simply don't matter.
**Approach:** Kafka gives **at-least-once** delivery; the HLL is **idempotent** on duplicate `hash(sessionId)` (adding the same element twice changes nothing), so retries and replays can't inflate the count. Flink **checkpoints** sketch state every few seconds; on crash it restores and replays from the committed offset, so at most one window is briefly re-derived — never double-counted.
**Why this is the move:** the expensive guarantee (exactly-once) is unnecessary because the data structure itself absorbs duplicates. We get correctness from idempotency, not from costly coordination.
**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** exactly-once stream processing adds coordination overhead and latency; here it buys nothing because HLL-add is idempotent — so we take cheap at-least-once and save the budget.

**✅ Failure matrix:**

| Scenario | What happens | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Heartbeat delivered twice | Same element added to HLL twice | No effect — idempotent |
| Flink restarts mid-window | Replays recent offsets | HLL absorbs replays; window self-heals |
| Redis loses a sketch | One stream's count blips | Flink re-publishes within a tick from its own state (source of truth) |
| Clock skew on clients | Window boundaries fuzzy | Server-stamped time is authoritative, not client time |

**🎙️ Script:** "I deliberately choose at-least-once delivery, not exactly-once, because HyperLogLog is idempotent — adding the same session twice does nothing. That means retries, Kafka replays, and Flink restarts can't inflate the count, so I get correctness for free instead of paying for expensive exactly-once coordination. Flink checkpoints the sketches every few seconds and replays from the committed offset on crash, so the worst case is one window briefly re-derived, never double-counted. Server-stamped time defines the windows, so flaky client clocks can't smear the boundaries."

**🧠 If they ask "what if you genuinely need exact counts (billing)?":** "Then this is the wrong system — I'd keep this approximate counter for the live display and run a separate, offline exactly-once batch job over the durable Kafka log for audit-grade numbers. Two systems, two guarantees, because the display and the ledger have different requirements."

## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost

**9A — Availability targets (what the "nines" mean):**
- **Read path (the displayed count): target 99.9% ("three nines") = ~8.7 hours/year of degradation total** — and "degraded" means a stale number, not an error. Mechanisms: **CDN** edge caching (serves a recent value if origin is unreachable), Redis **replicas**, and **multi-AZ** deployment — *multi-AZ means running in multiple data centres within one region so if one building loses power, another takes over automatically*.
- **Write/ingest path: target 99.95%** — losing heartbeats briefly just means the count lags slightly; Kafka's durability + multi-AZ brokers protect it.
- We express the *count* as best-effort-fresh: even at the worst moment we show a number, just older.

**9B — Per-dependency failure table:**

| Component | What breaks if it's down | How it degrades (what the USER sees) | Recovery action |
|---|---|---|---|
| API Gateway | Heartbeats can't ingest | Count freezes then slowly decays as windows expire; reads still work from cache | Multi-AZ gateways; clients retry with backoff |
| Kafka | Firehose can't buffer | Aggregation stalls; last counts served (stale) | Multi-AZ brokers, replication factor 3; auto-recover |
| Flink | No new aggregates | Counts frozen at last published value (stale, not wrong) | Restore from checkpoint, replay offsets |
| Redis | No published surface | Fan-out pushes last-known value; CDN serves cached | Replica failover; warm from Flink state |
| Fan-out tier | Pushes stop | Clients reconnect to other nodes; fall back to cached GET | Stateless nodes, autoscale, reconnect |
| CDN | Fallback reads hit origin | Slightly higher origin load; push path unaffected | Multi-CDN or origin shielding |
| Time-series DB | History writes fail | Live count unaffected; trend graph gaps | Buffer rollups, backfill on recovery |

The golden property: **every failure degrades to a *stale* number, never an error or a wrong number.** Liveness decays gracefully because counts are windowed.

**9C — RPO / RTO per data class (plain definitions first):**
*RPO (Recovery Point Objective) = how much data we're OK losing if it all crashes right now. RTO (Recovery Time Objective) = how long until the service works again.*

| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Live counts / sketches | Seconds (rebuildable) | < 30s | Ephemeral by design — Flink rebuilds sketches from Kafka in one window; nothing durable to lose |
| Heartbeat log (Kafka) | ≈ 0 within retention | < 1 min | Replication factor 3, multi-AZ; durable buffer |
| History rollups | ≤ 1 minute | Minutes | Append-only TS DB, replicated; backfill from Kafka if needed |

The beautiful part: the *live* state is intentionally disposable. We never restore counts — we **recompute** them from the durable log in one window. RPO/RTO are trivial because the source of truth is the replayable Kafka log, not the counters.

**9D — Cost breakdown:**
Top 3 drivers: (1) **Fan-out egress + connection servers** — holding tens of millions of persistent connections and pushing to them is the biggest line; *every pushed byte to every screen is metered*. (2) **Kafka + Flink compute** — the firehose ingest and stateful streaming cluster run hot at peak. (3) **CDN egress** for the fallback path. Rough order-of-magnitude at peak: low-to-mid five figures/month of dedicated infra for the counting system (a rounding error against the video CDN bill, which is the real platform cost). The single biggest optimization: **lengthen the heartbeat interval and coalesce pushes** — going from a 10s to a 30s heartbeat cuts the write firehose ~3x (5M→1.7M/s) for a barely-perceptible freshness cost; it's a direct cost dial.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "When things fail, the user keeps seeing a number — it just gets a little stale, because the count is windowed and degrades gracefully rather than erroring. I never restore live state; I recompute it from the durable Kafka log in a single window, so my recovery objectives are basically free. The biggest cost is holding and pushing to tens of millions of connections, and my best cost lever is the heartbeat interval — stretching it from ten to thirty seconds cuts the write load threefold for freshness no one will notice."

## 10. Trade-off Ledger

**🔀 Decision: Approximate (HyperLogLog) vs Exact (sets/DB)**
1. **What we chose and why:** HLL sketches — ±2% distinct count in ~12 KB, mergeable, idempotent. Counting at this scale is only affordable approximately.
2. **What we gave up:** exactness; we literally cannot tell you the precise number to the unit.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** like estimating a stadium crowd from the density of the stands instead of counting every head — far cheaper, plenty accurate for a sign.
4. **When this reverses:** if the count becomes billing/audit-grade (pay-per-viewer), we add a *separate* offline exactly-once batch over the Kafka log; the live display stays approximate.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "It's a display number, so I trade exactness for a 65,000x memory win and free mergeability — and if anyone ever needs exact numbers for billing, that's a separate offline job, not this hot path."

**🔀 Decision: Eventual (~10s) vs Strong consistency**
1. **What we chose and why:** eventual, windowed freshness — buys batching, caching, and push coalescing.
2. **What we gave up:** the number can trail reality by several seconds and lacks read-your-own-writes.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** like a scoreboard updated every few seconds rather than the instant each point is scored — nobody minds.
4. **When this reverses:** if a use case needs sub-second exact liveness (e.g. interactive auctions tied to concurrency), we'd push per-change and shrink windows at real cost.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "A few seconds stale is invisible on a 'watching now' badge, and that relaxation is what makes both the write and read sides affordable."

**🔀 Decision: Push fan-out vs Client polling**
1. **What we chose and why:** push over persistent connections so cost scales with streams, not viewers.
2. **What we gave up:** the operational burden of holding tens of millions of long-lived connections.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** one PA announcement to the whole stadium instead of everyone phoning the office for the score.
4. **When this reverses:** for tiny audiences or clients that can't hold sockets, a CDN-cached poll is simpler and we already keep it as the fallback.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Polling is ten million reads a second for one number, so I push — and I keep a cached GET as the simple fallback for clients that can't hold a socket."

**🔀 Decision: At-least-once + idempotent sketch vs Exactly-once stream processing**
1. **What we chose and why:** at-least-once delivery, because HLL-add is idempotent so duplicates are harmless.
2. **What we gave up:** the theoretical cleanliness of exactly-once.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** we don't pay guards to ensure each ticket is scanned exactly once, because scanning the same ticket twice changes nothing.
4. **When this reverses:** if we switched to a non-idempotent aggregate (e.g. summing watch-seconds for billing), we'd need exactly-once or careful dedup.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Exactly-once buys me nothing here because the sketch absorbs duplicates, so I take cheap at-least-once and spend the savings elsewhere."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The decisions I'd flag: I chose approximate over exact, eventual over strong, push over poll, and at-least-once over exactly-once. Every one of them trades a guarantee nobody needs on a live display number for enormous savings in memory, latency, and cost. And each reverses cleanly — make the number billing-grade, or sub-second, or non-idempotent, and I'd revisit that specific choice, usually by bolting on a separate system rather than making this hot path heavier."

## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**❓ "Two viewers, same person on phone and TV — one or two? And how do you de-dupe the same session heartbeating repeatedly?"**
**The mechanism:** the dedup key is sessionId (or userId if we want human-distinct). The HLL adds `hash(key)`; adding the same key repeatedly is idempotent, so a session heartbeating every 10s counts once. Phone+TV are two sessions unless we re-key on userId.
**🗣️ In plain words:** we count fingerprints, not pings — the same device pinging a hundred times is still one fingerprint.
**💬 One-liner:** "Distinctness comes from the sketch keyed on session; repeated heartbeats are idempotent, so they can't inflate the count."

**❓ "What happens when Flink (the aggregator) crashes mid-window?"**
**The mechanism:** Flink checkpoints sketch state every few seconds and tracks Kafka offsets; on crash it restores the last checkpoint and replays from the committed offset. HLL absorbs the replayed duplicates, so at most one window is briefly re-derived, never double-counted.
**🗣️ In plain words:** it picks up from its last save point and re-reads the recent firehose; since duplicates don't matter, nothing breaks.
**💬 One-liner:** "Restore from checkpoint, replay from offset — and because the sketch is idempotent, the replay is harmless."

**❓ "The cricket final is 90% of all viewers — isn't that one stream a hot-spot?"**
**The mechanism:** yes — a single hot key. We sub-shard that stream's key across N Flink tasks (`streamId#0..N`), each maintaining its own HLL, and merge the sketches for the stream's count. No single task owns the whole final.
**🗣️ In plain words:** we split the big crowd across several counters and add the counters up, so no one counter drowns.
**💬 One-liner:** "Sub-shard the hot stream's key across tasks and merge the sketches — the mergeability that gives me the global count also relieves the hot key."

**❓ "How do you serve the number to 50M screens without melting?"**
**The mechanism:** a push fan-out tier holds WebSocket/SSE connections and broadcasts coalesced deltas (≤1/s) read once per stream from Redis; cost scales with streams, not viewers. A 3s CDN-cached GET is the fallback. Polling (10M reads/s) is explicitly avoided.
**🗣️ In plain words:** one announcement to the whole stadium, not everyone calling in for the score.
**💬 One-liner:** "Push coalesced deltas over shared connections; polling would be ten million reads a second for one number."

**❓ "Can the count be wrong or drift over time?"**
**The mechanism:** it's approximate by design (±~2% from HLL) and windowed, so it self-corrects every window — there's no accumulating drift because each window is recomputed from current heartbeats, not incremented forever. Below ~1000 viewers we use an exact set.
**🗣️ In plain words:** it's a fresh estimate every few seconds, not a running tally that can slowly go bad.
**💬 One-liner:** "No drift — each window is recomputed fresh; the only error is HLL's bounded ±2%, invisible at millions."

**❓ "What stops someone from faking heartbeats to inflate the count?"**
**The mechanism:** sessionId is derived server-side from the auth token/connection, not trusted from the body; rate-limiting and per-account session caps bound how many distinct sessions one actor can mint. The count uses distinct sessions, so duplicate fake pings from one session do nothing.
**🗣️ In plain words:** you can't stuff the ballot box because each ballot is tied to a real logged-in session we issue, not one you can type in.
**💬 One-liner:** "Identity is server-derived and rate-limited, and distinct-counting means spamming one session changes nothing."

**❓ "Can the same viewer be counted twice across two streams in the global number?"**
**The mechanism:** the global count is a *merge* (union) of per-stream sketches, and HLL union de-dupes — a user on two streams contributes one element to the union. Summing integer per-stream counts would double-count; merging sketches does not.
**🗣️ In plain words:** we combine the fingerprints, not the totals, so a person on two screens is still one fingerprint globally.
**💬 One-liner:** "Global is a sketch union, not an integer sum, so cross-stream viewers are counted once."

**❓ "What's the most expensive part, and how would you cut cost?"**
**The mechanism:** holding and pushing to tens of millions of persistent connections is the top line, then Kafka/Flink compute. The biggest lever is the heartbeat interval: 10s→30s cuts the write firehose ~3x (5M→1.7M/s) for imperceptible freshness loss; coalescing pushes further cuts egress.
**🗣️ In plain words:** the costly part is talking to every screen; we talk less often and the bill drops a lot.
**💬 One-liner:** "Connections and egress dominate — stretching the heartbeat from 10 to 30 seconds is a 3x cost cut for freshness no one notices."

**❓ "How would you add per-minute history / a concurrency-over-time graph?"**
**The mechanism:** a tiny rollup writes `(streamId, minute) → count` from Redis into a time-series/columnar store (ClickHouse/Cassandra); the dashboard range-scans it. Write rate is trivial (per-minute per-stream), and it's fully decoupled from the live hot path.
**🗣️ In plain words:** snapshot the live number once a minute into a cheap history table and chart it.
**💬 One-liner:** "Per-minute rollups into a time-series store give the trend graph without touching the live path."

**❓ "What if you later need exact, audit-grade counts (e.g. for billing)?"**
**The mechanism:** keep this approximate system for the live display, and run a *separate* offline exactly-once batch over the durable Kafka log to produce audit-grade numbers. Two systems, two guarantees — don't burden the hot path with billing correctness.
**🗣️ In plain words:** the live sign stays a fast estimate; the official numbers come from a careful overnight job on the saved data.
**💬 One-liner:** "Display and ledger have different needs — I'd add an offline exact job on the Kafka log rather than make the live path exact."

**❓ "Single region or global, and how does the global number work across regions?"**
**The mechanism:** count locally per region (regional Flink + sketches), then ship the small per-region sketches to a central merge job that unions them for the global number. Sketches are ~12 KB, so cross-region merge traffic is negligible, and a region outage just drops its contribution.
**🗣️ In plain words:** each region counts its own crowd and mails a tiny summary to headquarters, which adds them up.
**💬 One-liner:** "Count regionally, merge tiny sketches centrally — global is a cheap union, and a dead region degrades it, doesn't break it."

> 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "This is a live 'watching now' counter for a streaming platform at cricket-final scale — about fifty million concurrent viewers turned into one fresh number per stream and one global number. The design splits into two halves. The read half is pure fan-out: counts live in Redis, a push tier broadcasts coalesced updates over WebSockets so cost scales with streams not viewers, and a short-lived CDN cache backs the polling fallback — because letting fifty million screens poll would be ten million reads a second for one number. The write half is the hard half: a five-million-heartbeat-a-second firehose lands on Kafka, and Flink folds each stream's heartbeats into a HyperLogLog sketch over a sliding window — the single mechanism that de-dupes repeat pings, forgets viewers who left, fits in twelve kilobytes, and merges, which is how I both relieve the hot cricket-final stream and compute the global count. The two halves connect through Redis and the event log: Flink publishes sketches, fan-out reads them. And every hard decision traces back to one principle — it's a display number, so I trade exactness and strong consistency for approximate, eventual, mergeable counting, and that single trade is what makes fifty million concurrent viewers affordable."
